question: What is the main purpose of the Adapter pattern?

options.text: To convert an incompatible interface to the target one without changing the wrapped object's source code
To add new functionality to an object through a wrapper
options.explanation: Adding functionality is the Decorator's job. Adapter only translates calls between incompatible interfaces

options.text: To control access to an object (caching, logging)
options.explanation: Access control is the Proxy's job. Adapter solves interface incompatibility

options.text: To simplify a complex system of multiple subsystems
options.explanation: Simplifying complex systems is the Facade's job. Adapter works with a single object, converting its interface

question: Why must a Decorator preserve the signature of the wrapped function?

options.text: So the calling code doesn't know about the decorator's existence — the decorator must be transparent
Because TypeScript doesn't allow changing signatures
options.explanation: TypeScript allows returning a function with a different signature — but this violates the decorator contract

options.text: For compatibility with TC39 class decorators
options.explanation: Functional decorators (wrappers) and TC39 class decorators (@decorator) are different things

options.text: To avoid runtime errors
options.explanation: Preserving the signature is primarily for compile-time safety and contract compliance. Runtime errors are a consequence, not the cause

question: How does a Facade differ from a God Object?

options.text: Facade delegates work to subsystems and coordinates them, while a God Object implements all logic internally
Facade has fewer methods than a God Object
options.explanation: Number of methods is not the defining factor. The key difference is that Facade delegates rather than implementing logic itself

options.text: A God Object is a Facade with more than 5 subsystems
options.explanation: A God Object is an anti-pattern where one class does everything itself. The number of subsystems doesn't turn a Facade into a God Object

options.text: Facade is a God Object with an interface
options.explanation: Facade is fundamentally different: it coordinates rather than containing business logic

question: Which types of Proxy are described in the theory?

options.text: Caching — stores call results
Logging — records all accesses
Protection — checks access rights
Virtual — defers creation of heavy objects
Transforming — converts data between formats
options.explanation: Converting data between formats is the Adapter's job, not Proxy's

question: Why is it important to use generics instead of `any` when creating Proxy in TypeScript?

options.text: To preserve type safety — TypeScript will know the types of methods and properties of the proxied object
Because the Proxy API doesn't accept the any parameter type
options.explanation: JavaScript Proxy accepts any object. Generics are needed for TypeScript typing, not runtime restrictions

options.text: To improve Proxy performance
options.explanation: Generics are erased at compilation and don't affect runtime performance

options.text: It's a mandatory ESLint requirement
options.explanation: While ESLint may warn about using any, this is a matter of architectural choice, not a linter requirement
